summary: Joel Spolsky announces Kiln Harmony, a major new version of Fog Creek's distributed version control system that lets developers use either Git or Mercurial interchangeably on the same repository. He argues that DVCS is the most important developer tool advancement of the decade and explains why commercial products like Kiln and GitHub add value on top of raw open-source tools. He positions Kiln as serving the corporate market (code reviews, access control) while GitHub serves open source (social networking, forking). The key technical achievement is that Kiln stores repos in both formats with perfect bidirectional translation, letting teams migrate gradually or mix tools freely.
key_points: Distributed version control is the single biggest improvement in developers' lives in the past decade, even though it's harder to use than centralized systems
Commercial DVCS products succeed by wrapping raw open-source tools in enterprise features — they're Cadillacs to open source's dune buggies
Kiln targets corporate workflows (code review, permissions, search) while GitHub targets open-source sociology (forking, pull requests, social profiles)
Fog Creek originally chose Mercurial as '1% better' than Git, but GitHub's ecosystem growth made Git support essential
Kiln Harmony stores every repo in both Git and Mercurial formats with perfect 1:1 reversible translation, letting teams mix tools freely
money_quotes: DVCS is such an important improvement over the previous generation of centralized version control (Subversion, CVS, etc.) that it's a required upgrade, even though it's honestly a bit harder to use.
They're dune buggies. Powerful, yes, and sufficient for a college project, but as it turns out, people buy Cadillacs, not dune buggies, to drive around in.
I think of Kiln as the corporate Lincoln Town Car, while GitHub is kind of a VW Minibus.
Since internal corporate projects have a very different sociology than open source projects, Kiln is very different than GitHub.
We decided Mercurial was about 1% better than Git and that's the way we went.
GitHub has taken the world by storm, creating an ecosystem around Git that more than makes up for its minor failings.
We could have done it the lazy way: support both kinds of repositories and make you choose which one to use. But we are not lazy.
If Joe in Accounting refuses to move, it doesn't matter. He can keep using Mercurial.
key_insight: Commercial developer tools succeed not by reinventing open-source technology but by wrapping it in the workflow-specific features that match how different communities — corporate teams versus open-source contributors — actually collaborate.
